figures.caption: FIG. 4. The total Berry curvature of the lowest two degenerate bands (left) and the next lowest degenerate bands (right), for the (Jvc, Jcc) = (2, 5) system shown in Fig. 3b. The curvature is concentrated around the avoided crossing, which occur along a ring of points in k-space.

FIG. 5. The energy gap between the lowest and second lowest sets of bands in the tetrahedral and canted tetrahedral band structures as Jcc and Jvc are varied. From left to right: the tetrahedral state, a deformation where the three downward-pointing spins are each canted 10◦ upwards in the z-direction, and a similar deformation but canted by 25◦. A negative gap (blue) denotes a metallic state. Each of the spin states have non-zero chirality, and the points in the gapped regions host a σxy = 4 e2/h QAH state. The gap size at a given point decreases as the magnetization increases, and furthermore the gap survives only for points where Jvc and Jcc are large enough to counteract the diminishing magnitudes of the alternating spin sums.

FIG. 7. The top figures show several (Jvc, Jcc) = (2, 5) tetrahedral band structures as the gap between the itinerant conduction and valence bands, ∆ϵ ≡ ϵc − ϵv, is tuned. The lowest pair of bands in the left and right structures have Chern number C = 4 and C = 0, respectively. The middle band structure is at the critical point between the topological phases, which occurs at ∆ϵ⋆ ≈ 16.8. The bottom panel shows the summed Chern number for each pair of degenerate bands as ∆ϵ is tuned.

FIG. 1. (a) Triangular lattice with a 4-site magnetic unit cell with sites {A,B,C,D}. We set the lattice constant to unity in subsequent calculations and plots. (b) First Brillouin zone of the triangular lattice. The low-energy valence and conduction states in the model lie around Γ and the M points {Ma,Mb,Mc}, respectively; the circle and ellipses depict cross sections of the itinerant electron dispersions, given in Eq. (5). (c) An example of an all-out tetrahedral spin configuration, in which each spin points outwards from the center of a tetrahedron. The configuration forms a chiral spin state with zero net magnetization.

FIG. 2. The q-ordering, average scalar chirality, and average magnetization of the ground states as Jvc and, (a) Jcc or (b) an external field h⃗ = hz ẑ, are varied. The other couplings are held fixed to (Jv, Jc) = (2, 4) in both cases and Jcc = 6 in (b). Black and red lines denote first and second order phase transitions, respectively. In both diagrams, the states with largest average chirality, zero magnetization, and 3q characterization are tetrahedral. Other chiral and non-chiral 3q states with net magnetization appear as well. Illustrations of the phases are shown in (i)-(vii); the red arrows identify when three of the spins are degenerate. Here, the Distorted Tetrahedral state is given by lowering (raising) one leg of the tetrahedral configuration while raising (lowering) the other two to a lesser degree, while the Canted Tetrahedral I and II have all three legs cant upwards equally towards the field. In the Canting Pairs state, the upwards and downwards pairs cant at different angles and are perpendicular, e.g., one can think of the upper pair as projecting onto the x-axis and the lower pair onto the y-axis.

FIG. 6. The q-ordering, average scalar chirality, and average magnetization of the ground states as Jvc and, (a) Jcc or (b) an external field h⃗ = hz ẑ, are varied. The other couplings are held fixed to (Jv, Jc) = (0, 0) in both cases and Jcc = 6 in (b). Black and red lines denote first and second order phase transitions, respectively. Tetrahedral states exist at hz = 0 and transition to Canting Pairs at finite field. The orientation of the Distorted Tetrahedral state is the time-reversed version of the orientation in Fig. 2i. The Distorted Y-shape state is obtained by rotating (along the dashed circle) the two equally-canted legs of the D.T. state into the plane of the other two spins, hence forming a coplanar state like the Y-shape but with differing separation angles.

FIG. 3. The lowest two sets of degenerate bands in k-space for the tetrahedral spin state. The horizontal axis is a line along the M⃗a ≡ (π, π/ √ 3) direction through k = 0. For both parameter choices the band inversions occur, which opens a gap and generates a Chern number of C = 4 in the lowest pair of degenerate bands. In (a), one can place the Fermi surface (dashed blue line) anywhere between the gap in the avoided crossings to achieve an insulating state. For the other parameter choice in (b), the upper bands lower into this gap. However, since the Berry curvature is concentrated around the inversion points (Fig. 4), the metallic state with the illustrated Fermi surface appears to have a Hall conductivity of σxy = 4 e2/h.
